segments.purgatorio-1-g61: Mostrata ho lui tutta la gente ria; uses a poetic word order: the past participle mostrata appears before the auxiliary ho for emphasis and meter. The participle mostrata agrees in gender/number with the direct object tutta la gente (feminine singular), so it is feminine; lui functions as the person to whom the showing was made (to him).
segments.purgatorio-1-g62: e ora intendo mostrar quelli spirti che purgan sé sotto la tua balìa. Here intendo is the present indicative of intendere meaning “I intend/mean.” Mostrar is the (poetic/truncated) infinitive ‘to show’ that follows intendo. Quelli spirti = “those spirits.” Purgan is an archaic present plural (modern purgano) meaning “they purge” and sé is the reflexive pronoun (“themselves”); sotto la tua balìa means ‘under your sway/charge.’
segments.purgatorio-1-g63: Com’ io l’ho tratto, is a compressed clause: Com’ = come/as, l’ is the object clitic lo (him) attached before the auxiliary (l’ho), and tratto is the past participle of trarre meaning ‘brought/led/rescued.’ So the clause means ‘As I have brought him,’ with the normal auxiliary + participle construction appearing in a poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-1-g64: saria lungo a dirti; uses the archaic conditional saria (modern sarebbe) meaning ‘it would be’; lungo a dirti is an idiom ‘long to tell you’ (lungo = lengthy, dirti = to tell you). The infinitive dirti is the verb dire with the clitic ti attached.
segments.purgatorio-1-g65: de l’alto scende virtù che m’aiuta conducerlo a vederti e a udirti. De l’alto = from on high (de is an archaic form of di/da). Scende is present tense ‘descends’ and virtù here means a divine power/virtue that follows in a relative clause (che m’aiuta = that helps me). Conducerlo is the infinitive conducere with the object clitic lo attached (to bring/lead him), and a vederti / a udirti are purpose infinitives ‘to see you’ and ‘to hear you’ with clitic ti.
segments.purgatorio-1-g66: Or ti piaccia gradir la sua venuta: Or = now; piaccia is a present subjunctive used impersonally here (‘may it please’), with ti marking the person affected (‘you’): ‘may it please you.’ Gradir is the infinitive (poetic truncated form of gradire) ‘to accept/receive gladly,’ so the whole phrase is a polite request: ‘Now, may you be pleased to receive his coming.’
segments.purgatorio-1-g67: libertà va cercando, uses the periphrasis andare + gerund (va cercando) to express a continuing action ‘goes about seeking’ or simply ‘seeks.’ Libertà is the feminine noun ‘freedom,’ the object of the search.
segments.purgatorio-1-g68: ch’è sì cara, is a short relative clause: che (contracted to ch’) = which/that, è = is, sì = so/very, cara = dear/precious (feminine to agree with libertà).
segments.purgatorio-1-g69: come sa chi per lei vita rifiuta. come = as; sa is present ‘knows’ (from sapere); chi = he who / whoever (subject of rifiuta); per lei = ‘for her’ (liberty personified); vita rifiuta = ‘refuses life’ — word order is poetic but the sense is ‘as he who for her refuses his life knows.’
segments.purgatorio-1-g70: Tu ’l sai = Tu lo sai. ’l is the clitic form of lo (it) attached with an apostrophe; sai is present indicative, 2nd person singular (you know). The subject tu is explicit for emphasis; word order is subject + clitic object + verb.
segments.purgatorio-1-g71: ché non ti fu per lei amara in Utica la morte = because the death in Utica was not bitter to you on her account. Ché is a causal conjunction 'because/for'; non negates the verb. Ti is a dative/ethical dative 'to you'; fu is passato remoto of essere 'was'; amara is a predicative adjective characterizing la morte (the subject, deliberately placed after the predicate in poetic word order); per lei = 'on account of her / for her'; in Utica gives the place.
segments.purgatorio-1-g72: ove lasciasti la vesta ch’al gran dì sarà sì chiara = where you left the garment that on the great day will be so bright. Ove = where; lasciasti is passato remoto, 2nd person singular 'you left/left behind' (poetic past of lasciare); la vesta is the direct object 'the robe/garment'; ch’ = che, relative 'which/that'; al = a + il 'on the / at the'; gran dì = the 'great day' (i.e. Judgment Day); sarà is future 3rd sing. 'will be'; sì chiara = 'so bright/clear'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g41: Son is the poetic contraction of sono (present indicative of essere) and here introduces a question by inversion (verb before subject). 'le leggi d’abisso' is a genitive phrase ('the laws/rules of the abyss'); d’ is the elided preposition di. così rotte is an adjectival phrase: rotte is the feminine plural past participle of rompere used adjectivally and agrees with leggi, and così modifies the degree ('so/thus broken').
segments.purgatorio-1-g42: o is the disjunctive 'or' introducing an alternative/question. è mutato is composed of the present of essere plus the past participle mutato and functions like a perfect/passive ('has changed' or 'has been changed'); Dante places the predicate before its subject (inversion). in ciel is a locative prepositional phrase ('in heaven'), and novo consiglio is the subject in postposition (novo = archaic form of nuovo).
segments.purgatorio-1-g43: che is the subordinating conjunction 'that', introducing the following explanatory/restrictive clause (equivalent to 'that' or 'so that' in this rhetorical question).
segments.purgatorio-1-g44: dannati is a vocative/nominalized adjective meaning 'the damned' addressed to the sinners; the comma placement marks it as a direct address. Grammatically it is plural and stands outside the clause's subject-verb order as the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-1-g45: venite is second-person plural present indicative of venire ('you come' / 'are you coming'), here used as a direct question to those addressed. a le is the older split form of the modern contracted alla ('to the'); mie is the feminine plural possessive agreeing with grotte; grotte is the feminine plural noun 'caves/grottoes', the goal of motion governed by a.
segments.purgatorio-1-g46: Full stop punctuation marking the end of the speech/question; Dante's punctuation separates the quoted speech from what follows.
segments.purgatorio-1-g47: Lo is the definite article (archaic form) used before duca; duca mio literally 'my duke' here means 'my guide' (Virgil). allor is an adverb 'then'; mi is the indirect/object pronoun 'me'; diè is the archaic third-person singular past tense (diede/fece) 'gave' and in the idiom diè di piglio ('gave a grasp') means 'seized'; di piglio is a noun phrase 'of a grip' describing the manner of seizure.
segments.purgatorio-1-g48: The clause begins with e con ... repeated for rhythmic emphasis: 'and with words and with hands and with reverent signs/gestures' (con + plural nouns). reverenti modifies cenni and is an adjective in the plural agreeing with cenni. mi fé is archaic past 'he made/caused (to me)' (fé = fece), so 'he caused (to) me' — here meaning he caused my limbs/eyelid to move; le gambe e 'l ciglio are the direct objects: le gambe (my legs) and 'l (il) ciglio (the eyelid/brow), with possessive understood from the preceding mi.
segments.purgatorio-1-g90: Ma is an adversative conjunction ('but'). Se introduces a conditional clause ('if'); here it governs the whole subordinate clause that follows. Donna del ciel is the subject phrase ('a lady of Heaven') placed before the verbs for emphasis; the clitic ti ('you') stands before the verbs and is the direct/indirect object of both verbs. Move e regge are two present‑tense, 3rd person singular verbs joined by e ('and'), both agreeing with donna and both taking the clitic ti.
segments.purgatorio-1-g91: Come tu di' is an elliptical parenthetical: come ('as') + explicit subject pronoun tu for emphasis; di' is a poetic contraction (apostrophe) of dici ('you say') used to fit the verse. The order is normal emphatic Italian: 'as you say'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g92: Non c’è is the impersonal/existential construction 'there is not' (ci + è). Mestier is an archaic form meaning 'need' or 'necessity'; it takes the following noun as what is unnecessary. Lusinghe is a plural noun 'flatteries/flattery' (here 'flattery' in general). The colon marks that an explanation or consequence follows.
segments.purgatorio-1-g93: Bastisi is an archaic/impersonal form based on bastare ('to suffice') with a reflexive/impersonal -si; the sense is 'it is enough' or 'it will suffice'. Ben is an intensifier ('well/enough'). Che introduces the subordinate clause that follows. Per lei means 'for her' or 'on her account/sake'. Mi richegge: richegge is a rare/poetic verb form in the 3rd person singular present and mi is the first‑person clitic object; together they mean roughly 'she recalls/calls me back' or 'she turns me back toward (the place/person)', so the whole clause says 'that for her she calls me back.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g94: Va is the 2nd-person singular imperative of andare — a direct command “go.” Dunque is an adverb following the imperative meaning “then/therefore,” reinforcing the command.
segments.purgatorio-1-g95: E is the coordinating conjunction “and.” Fa is the 2nd-person singular imperative of fare used idiomatically here (“see to it” / “make (that)”), introducing a subordinate clause with che + (subjunctive) to express a commanded result. Che introduces the subordinate clause; tu is the explicit subject “you.” Costui is a demonstrative pronoun “this fellow/this man.” Ricinghe is a subjunctive/command-form of ricingere meaning “gird / bind” (Dante’s spelling reflects older morphology). D’un = di + un, “of a / with a.” Giunco is “reed”; schietto is an adjective here meaning “straight/plain” describing the reed. The second che introduces another subordinate clause (parallel): li is an enclitic pronoun here functioning as the indirect object “to him” (Dante’s li = modern gli); lavi is an imperative/indicative form of lavare meaning “wash,” and ’l is the contracted article “the” before viso (“face”).
segments.purgatorio-1-g96: Sì ch’ (sì che) is a standard connective meaning “so that” or “with the result that.” Ogne (ogni) is an archaic form meaning “every.” Sucidume is a poetic/older form meaning “filth/stains” (Dante spelling); quindi means “from there/then” (here: “afterwards/thereby”), and stinghe is a present-tense verb (from stingere/stingo) meaning “extinguish / blot out / wipe away.” The clause states the intended result of the previous actions.
segments.purgatorio-1-g97: Ché = perché/ché here introduces the reason (“for” / “because”). Non is the negation. Si is the impersonal/reflexive si used with converria to form an impersonal conditional idea (“it would be fitting”), and converria is the conditional form (historical/orthographic) of converire/converrebbe meaning “to be fitting / proper.” The phrase gives the motive for washing and binding.
segments.purgatorio-1-g98: L’ occhio = the eye; surpriso is a past participle/adjectival form meaning “surprised / caught off-guard / clouded” (used adjectivally here). D’ + alcuna = “by some / of some”; nebbia is “mist/fog.” Grammatically this participial phrase modifies the subject and explains the state in which it would be improper to proceed.
segments.purgatorio-1-g99: Andar is the infinitive “to go.” Dinanzi = “in front of / before.” Al is the contraction of a + il, “to the.” Primo is “first/foremost.” Ministro here means “minister/official/agent” (someone in authority). The infinitive clause gives the action that would be improper.
segments.purgatorio-1-g100: Ch’ è = che è, “who/which is.” Di introduces membership/association (“of”). Quei = “those / that group.” Di paradiso = “of Paradise” (i.e., belonging to the heavenly order). This relative clause identifies the minister as belonging to those of Paradise.
segments.purgatorio-1-g101: Questa is the demonstrative “this.” Isoletta is a diminutive noun “little island / islet.” Intorno = “around.” Ad imo ad imo is a repetitive, emphatic phrase meaning “to the bottom / to the very depths” (Dante’s doubling is rhetorical emphasis). Word order is normal for a descriptive opening.
segments.purgatorio-1-g102: Là giù colà are stacked adverbs of place — “there, down there, yonder” — placing the action. Dove introduces a relative clause “where.” La is the feminine direct object pronoun referring back to the islet; batte is present-tense of battere meaning “strikes” (the wave striking the islet); l’ onda = “the wave.” The sequence describes the physical location where the wave hits the islet.
segments.purgatorio-1-g103: Porta here is a verb (3rd-person singular present of portare) meaning “carries / bears.” Di introduces the material or content: giunchi = “reeds.” Sovra = “upon / over.” ’l (the contracted il) molle = “soft”; limo = “mud/silt.” The clause says the islet supports reeds on its soft mud.
segments.purgatorio-1-g104: Null’ altra = nulla altra, “no other.” Pianta = “plant.” Che introduces a relative clause in the subjunctive: facesse is imperfect subjunctive of fare, “that would make/put forth” (here: “produce”), fronda = “foliage/leafy branch.” O = “or.” Indurasse is the imperfect subjunctive of indurare “to harden / become woody”; together the clause says no other plant that would produce leaves or harden (become woody) grows there.
segments.purgatorio-1-g105: Vi = “there” (locative adverb). Puote is an archaic form of poter (può) meaning “can / is able.” Aver vita = “have life” = “live.” The clause states that nothing else can live there.
segments.purgatorio-1-g106: Però ch’ = perché, “because.” A le percosse = “to the blows / against the beatings” (the waves’ impact). Non seconda: seconda here is from secondare (to follow/withstand/respond); with non it means “does not respond/endure.” The line gives the reason — plants do not survive the repeated strikes.
segments.purgatorio-1-g107: Poscia = “afterwards / then.” Non sia is a present-subjunctive clause used here with a prohibitive/optative force: “let it not be” / “do not make your return from here.” Di qua = “from here.” Vostra = “your.” Reddita (reddita) functions as a noun/participial noun meaning “return,” so the phrase forbids returning by this route.
segments.purgatorio-1-g108: Lo sol: lo is the article; sol here designates the Sun (Dante’s lo sol). Vi is an indirect-object or locative clitic here meaning “to you” / “to you there” (it marks the person shown to). Mosterrà is future tense of mostrare “will show.” The clause promises that the Sun will show them the way. (Note: sol also has the adverbial sense “only,” but here context and article mark it as the Sun.)
segments.purgatorio-1-g109: Che = which/that. Surge is present of surgere “rises” (the Sun rising); omai = “now / by now.” The relative clause identifies the sun as the one that is rising at that moment.
segments.purgatorio-1-g110: Prendere is an infinitive meaning “to take” or “to approach” (here: to take the mountain by a route). Il monte = “the mountain.” A più lieve salita = “by a more gentle ascent” (a + noun expresses the route/means); più lieve is comparative ‘more gentle / easier’, salita = “ascent.” The clause gives the guidance the sun will show.
segments.purgatorio-1-g73: Non son is the older/poetic form of non sono (‘are not’); the negation non precedes the verb. li editti etterni is the plural definite subject (‘the eternal edicts’), with the archaic spelling etterni. per noi guasti places the prepositional phrase after the predicate: per noi = “for us” (affecting us) and guasti (past-participle/adjectival use) means ‘ruined/annulled’ here.
segments.purgatorio-1-g74: ché introduces a causal clause (‘for/because’). questi here is a demonstrative used as a nominative pronoun (‘this one’/‘he’); vive is the present indicative third-person singular ‘lives’. The coordinate clause e Minòs me non lega follows: Minòs is subject, me is the direct object placed before the negative + verb (me non lega = ‘does not bind me’). Lega is present indicative ‘binds/ties’.
segments.purgatorio-1-g75: ma = ‘but’. son here is 1st singular (son = sono) ‘I am’ (= ‘I belong’); del cerchio = of the circle/realm. ove introduces a relative clause ‘where’; son li occhi casti is an inverted order with verb before subject: 'there are the chaste eyes' (son = sono, li occhi = the eyes). di Marzia tua is a genitive phrase ‘of your Marzia’ modifying occhi.
segments.purgatorio-1-g76: che is the relative ‘who/which’ referring back to occhi (the eyes). ’n is the contracted/inverted form of in (poetic contraction) and vista here means ‘in sight’ / ‘at the sight of you’; ancor = still. ti priega is present indicative ‘beseeches/asks you’ (ti = you as direct object placed before the verb). Together the clause says the eyes, when they see you, still beg you.
segments.purgatorio-1-g77: o is the vocative interjection ‘O’ addressing someone directly (not the conjunction ‘or’). santo is an adjective modifying petto; petto (‘breast’) is used vocatively/metaphorically for the heart or inner self. The short phrase is an apostrophic address: “O holy breast.”
segments.purgatorio-1-g78: che begins a subordinate clause introduced by the pleading (the eyes beseech). per tua = ‘for your sake’ (per + possessive) with archaic word order. la is the feminine direct-object pronoun ‘her’ (referring to Marzia) placed before the verb; tegni is the present subjunctive or optative sense of tegnere (che tu tegni = ‘that you keep/hold her’), used in a request/desire clause after priega.
segments.purgatorio-1-g79: per lo suo amore = ‘for her love / for the sake of her love’ (lo + possessive is an older/poetic article+possessive order). adunque = ‘therefore/then’. a noi ti piega is a plea/imperative construction: ti (reflexive/accusative) + piega (2nd-person singular imperative or exhortative ‘bend/turn yourself’) meaning ‘so incline yourself toward us’. The whole line asks that, for her love, he incline himself to them.
segments.purgatorio-1-g80: Lasciane andar per li tuoi sette regni; = Lasciane (lascía + ne) is an imperative/inflected form meaning 'let them go' or 'allow them to go' with the enclitic ne referring to 'them' (or 'of them'); andar is the infinitive 'to go'; per li tuoi sette regni = 'through your seven kingdoms' (per + plural definite article li, poetic form of modern i/ i tuoi).
segments.purgatorio-1-g81: grazie riporterò di te a lei, = grazie (pl.) 'favors / thanks' is the object; riporterò is future 1st sing. 'I will bring back / I will report'; di te = 'from you' or 'concerning you' (shows the source/whose favors); a lei = 'to her'. The phrase states the speaker's promise to carry/relate the gratitude to the lady.
segments.purgatorio-1-g82: se d’esser mentovato là giù degni. = se = if; d’esser is the contracted di + esser (infinitive 'to be'); mentovato is past participle 'mentioned' (i.e. 'to be mentioned'); là giù = 'down there' (below, in the lower place); degni is predicate adjective 'worthy' in the plural, agreeing with grazie (the favors) — 'if they are worthy to be mentioned down there.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g83: «Marzïa piacque tanto a li occhi miei mentre ch’i’ fu’ di là», = Marzïa is the proper name; piacque is passato remoto 3rd sing. of piacere 'pleased' (Italian idiom: 'X pleased me' = 'I liked X', constructed here as 'pleased my eyes'); tanto = 'so much'; a li occhi miei = 'to my eyes' = 'to my sight / to me'; mentre = 'while'; ch’i’ fu’ = che io fui (passato remoto) 'I was'; di là = 'over there'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g84: diss’ elli allora, = diss' is an archaic/poetic form of disse (passato remoto 'he said'), often shown with an apostrophe in Dante; elli is an emphatic/old-fashioned third-person pronoun 'he' used as subject; allora = 'then'. The sequence is the narrator's tag: 'he said then.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g85: «che quante grazie volse da me, = che = that; quante = 'how many / as many as'; grazie = 'favors / thanks' (here 'favours'); volse is passato remoto 3rd sing. of volgere used idiomatically 'volse da me' = 'turned to me', i.e. 'asked of me' or 'wished from me'; da me = 'from me'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g86: fei. = fei is the archaic / poetic passato remoto 1st person singular of fare (modern feci) 'I did / I made' — here 'I did (them)' = 'I granted them'. The verb stands alone as the conclusion: 'I did (what she asked).'
segments.purgatorio-1-g87: Or che di là dal mal fiume dimora, = Or che = 'now that' (introducing a reason); di là dal mal fiume = 'over there beyond the evil/bad river' (di là = on the other side; dal = da + il 'from the'); dimora is 3rd person singular present 'dwells / remains' (dimorare) describing where 'he' now is.
segments.purgatorio-1-g88: più muover non mi può, = 'più' here means 'any longer'; muover is the infinitive 'to move' used with the modal può; non negates; mi is the object 'me'; può is 3rd person singular present of potere 'can / is able to' — together 'he can no longer move me.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g89: per quella legge che fatta fu quando me n’usci’ fora. = per quella legge 'because / by that law'; che fatta fu = relative clause 'which was made' (fatta fu is passive inversion of fu fatta, passato remoto, poetic word order); quando me n’usci’ = 'when I went out' (me n'uscii = me + ne + uscii, 'I came out of it/from there' in passato remoto); fora is an archaic/emphatic element reinforcing 'out' (poetic particle). The clause explains the legal/ritual rule that prevents further movement.
segments.purgatorio-1-g32: Li raggi de le quattro luci sante fregiavan sì la sua faccia di lume: the phrase has the subject 'Li raggi' (the rays) followed by the imperfect verb 'fregiavan' (fregiavano) in 3rd plural — an archaic poetic ending — meaning 'they adorned'. 'De le quattro luci sante' is a genitival phrase 'of the four holy lights', and 'sì ... di lume' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'so/with such effect ... with light' (here 'di' expresses the means, 'with/by').
segments.purgatorio-1-g33: ch’i’ ’l vedea come ’l sol fosse davante: 'ch’' is the contracted 'che' introducing a result/comparative clause; 'i’' is the enclitic/poetic form of 'io' (I) as subject; '’l' immediately after it is the object clitic 'lo' (= him/it) meaning 'I saw him'. 'Vedea' is the imperfect 'I saw' (archaic/poetic form of 'vedevo'), and 'come' introduces a simile 'as/like'. '’l sol fosse davante' is a subordinate clause with the imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' ('were') used in the simile 'as if the sun were before (him)'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g34: 'Chi siete voi' is a direct interrogative 'Who are you?'. The relative 'che' links that question to the following verb phrase. 'contro al cieco fiume' uses 'contro' + article ('al') meaning 'against/at the blind river' (here indicating movement in relation to the river, i.e. 'having fled the river'), and the verbal cluster 'fuggita avete' places the past participle 'fuggita' before the auxiliary 'avete' for metrical/poetic order, giving 'you have fled/escaped'. The direct object is 'la pregione etterna' = 'the eternal prison.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g35: A single comma marking the pause before the speech tag; it separates the quoted question from the speaker's reply and has no lexical content.
segments.purgatorio-1-g36: diss’ el,: 'diss’' is the elided poetic form of 'disse' (he said) and 'el' is the archaic subject clitic 'egli' (he). The verb precedes the subject clitic here, a common poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-1-g37: movendo quelle oneste piume.: 'movendo' is the present participle/gerund 'moving' indicating a simultaneous action; it governs the following noun phrase 'quelle oneste piume' ('those honourable/decent feathers'), where 'oneste' describes the modest/pious quality of the feathers (i.e. the angel's wings).
segments.purgatorio-1-g38: «Chi v’ha guidati,: 'Chi' = 'Who' (interrogative). 'v’' is the clitic 'vi' (you) attached before the auxiliary; 'ha' is the third-person singular perfect auxiliary 'has'; 'guidati' is the past participle 'guided' agreeing in number/gender with the clitic object (you). The structure is literally 'Who has guided you'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g39: o che vi fu lucerna,: 'o' = 'or' offering an alternative question; here 'che' functions interrogatively ('what'), so the phrase asks 'or what was ...'. 'vi' = 'to/for you' (dative/benefactive), and 'fu' is the perfect 'was'. The clause asks 'or what was for you a lamp/light' — i.e. 'what served as a light for you'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g40: uscendo fuor de la profonda notte che sempre nera fa la valle inferna?: 'uscendo' is a gerund 'coming out', 'fuor de' = 'out of', so 'coming out of the deep night'. The relative 'che' introduces 'which always makes the infernal valley black' — 'fa' is present 'makes' and 'la valle inferna' is the object ('the infernal valley'). Note the archaic prepositional forms (fuor de) and adjective-noun order 'valle inferna'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g17: I’ is a poetic contraction of io (“I”). Mi is the reflexive object used with volsi. Volsi is the first-person singular passato remoto of volgere/voltare meaning “I turned.” A man destra is an archaic/poetic word order and truncation (man = mano) meaning “to the right (hand).”
segments.purgatorio-1-g18: E = “and.” Puosi is a past (passato remoto) 1st-person form of porre/porre (here “I set/placed”), used in the idiom puorre mente = “to direct/set one's mind.” Mente = “mind/thought.” A l’altro polo = prepositional phrase “to the other pole,” with l’ = the article and altro polo = the opposite pole.
segments.purgatorio-1-g19: E = “and.” Vidi is passato remoto of vedere (“I saw”). Quattro stelle = direct object “four stars.” Non viste mai = non + past participle viste (agreeing with stelle) + mai = “never seen.” Fuor ch’a = archaic phrasing (fuor = fuori/except; ch’ = che) meaning “except that/by” and a la prima gente = “by/to the first people” (here = ‘by the first generation’).
segments.purgatorio-1-g20: Goder is an infinitive ('to enjoy') used impersonally as the thing that seems; pareva is the imperfect third-person singular of parere (“seemed”). ’l ciel (the subject) follows the verb in poetic inversion. Di lor fiammelle = “of/their little flames,” with lor = loro (their).
segments.purgatorio-1-g21: Oh is a vocative interjection. Settentrïonal = northern; vedovo = “widowed” or bereft (metaphorical, bereft of the stars); sito = “site” or “place.” The whole phrase is a direct apostrophe addressing the northern place.
segments.purgatorio-1-g22: Poi che = conjunction meaning “since/now that.” Privato is a past participle used adjectivally (“deprived”). Se’ is an archaic form equivalent to sei (“you are”). Di mirar = 'of seeing' (di + infinitive mirar). Quelle = demonstrative “those” (the stars). The clause addresses the place, saying it is deprived of seeing them.
segments.purgatorio-1-g23: Com’ is contraction of come (“as/when”). Io is the subject. Da loro sguardo = “from their gaze.” Fui partito is a compound past (fui + partito) describing having parted/been separated — literally “I was parted/left” from their sight; partito is the past participle of partire.
segments.purgatorio-1-g24: Un poco = “a little.” Me volgendo is a gerund (volgendo) with reflexive me: “turning myself.” A l’altro polo = “toward the other pole”; the gerund describes a simultaneous slight turn.
segments.purgatorio-1-g25: Là = “there.” Onde is a relative/adverbial element meaning “whence/where” or “from which” (archaic usage). ’l Carro = the Chariot/Wain (a constellation). Già = “already.” Era sparito is the past perfect/imperfect auxiliary + past participle meaning “had disappeared” (the Chariot was already gone).
segments.purgatorio-1-g26: Vidi is passato remoto of vedere (“I saw”). Presso di me = “near me” (presso + di + me). Un veglio solo = “an old man alone,” with veglio = archaic form meaning an old man and solo = alone.
segments.purgatorio-1-g27: Degno di = “worthy of.” Tanta reverenza = “such great reverence.” In vista here means “in appearance” or “to the sight,” so the phrase qualifies the old man as deserving visible reverence.
segments.purgatorio-1-g28: Che introduces a relative clause modifying the previous phrase. Più non = “no longer/any longer.” Dee is an archaic form of deve (present-tense modal ‘must/ought/ owes’). A padre alcun figliuolo = “to any father (no) son”; in context the clause says the reverence is greater than what any son ought to owe to a father (i.e. it exceeds normal filial duty). Word order and negative alcun are archaic.
segments.purgatorio-1-g29: Lunga la barba is a fronted adjective + noun (“long the beard”) in poetic inversion. E di pel bianco mista = “and mixed with white hair,” where pel = pelo (hair) and mista agrees with barba (mixed). Portava is the imperfect third-person singular of portare = “he wore/was carrying,” the main verb placed after the fronted object.
segments.purgatorio-1-g30: A’ (a + apostrophe) = a = “to/like” used with simigliante; suoi capelli = “his own hair”; simigliante = “resembling/like.” The phrase says the beard was similar to his hair.
segments.purgatorio-1-g31: De’ is a contraction of di (of/from) + the article; quai is an archaic relative (like cui/which) meaning “from which.” Cadeva is the imperfect of cadere (“fell” / “was falling”), describing that from those hairs a doppia lista (a double strip/band) fell down al petto = “to/on the chest.” The word order places the relative phrase before the verb.
segments.purgatorio-1-g49: Poscia is an adverbial, archaic/poetic for 'then' or 'afterwards'. Rispuose is the passato remoto (past) of rispondere/rispuogere ('answered'); the subject pronoun lui follows the verb for emphasis ('he answered').
segments.purgatorio-1-g50: Da me non venni is an idiomatic negated past: da + me here conveys 'by myself' / 'of my own accord' and venni is the passato remoto of venire ('I came'). The negative particle non negates the whole clause.
segments.purgatorio-1-g51: Donna scese del ciel: donna is the poetic 'lady' (not merely a neutral 'woman'), scese is the passato remoto of scendere ('descended'), and del is the contraction di + il; ciel is the poetic form of cielo ('heaven').
segments.purgatorio-1-g52: Per li cui prieghi is a relative phrase: per ('through/by') + li (article) + cui ('whose'), so 'through whose prayers'. De la mia compagnia = 'of my company/group'. Costui ('this one') names the person affected, and sovvenni is a past form of sovvenire meaning 'to help / succor' (here indicating that help/succour came thanks to those prayers).
segments.purgatorio-1-g53: Ma da ch’è tuo voler introduces a concessive/casual clause: da ch'è ('since it is') + tuo voler ('your will/desire'). Che governs a subordinate clause with the present subjunctive si spieghi ('be explained') — si here is the impersonal/reflexive particle making the verb passive/impersonal ('be explained'). Di nostra condizion = 'about/of our condition', and com’ ell’ è vera = 'as it is true' (com' = come; ell' = ella/it referring back to 'condizion').
segments.purgatorio-1-g54: Esser non puote il mio che a te si nieghi is a somewhat inverted, archaic construction. Esser is the infinitive 'to be' used as the complement to puote (archaic form of può, 'can'); il mio is an elliptical nominal ('my [part / duty / self]'), and the relative clause che a te si nieghi ('that deny itself to you') uses si as a reflexive/passive marker and nieghi in the present subjunctive. Overall: 'my [part] cannot be such that it denies itself to you.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g55: Questi non vide mai l’ultima sera: Questi = 'this one/he' (demonstrative pronoun), vide is passato remoto of vedere ('saw'), and non...mai = 'never'. L'ultima sera ('the last evening') is a poetic expression.
segments.purgatorio-1-g56: ma per la sua follia le fu sì presso: per indicates cause ('because of' / 'through'), la sua follia = 'his folly', le is a dative/indirect-clitic ('to it/to her') referring back to l'ultima sera, fu is passato remoto of essere ('was'), and sì presso is an adverbial phrase 'so near / almost'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g57: che introduces a result clause ('so that'), molto poco tempo = 'very little time', and a volger era is an archaic periphrasis: volger ('to turn / to pass') with the copula era ('was') gives 'there was little time for it to pass / little time remained'. Word order is inverted compared with modern prose.
segments.purgatorio-1-g58: Sì com’ io dissi = 'as I said': Sì come (contracted com') introduces a comparison/recall, and dissi is passato remoto 'I said'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g59: fui mandato ad esso per lui campare: fui mandato is a passive passato remoto ('I was sent'), ad esso = 'to him/it' (dative indication of recipient), and per + lui + campare expresses purpose/benefit ('for/so that I might live by him' or 'to live through him'); campare is the infinitive 'to live / survive'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g60: e non lì era altra via che questa per la quale i’ mi son messo: 'there was no other way there than this one, by which I set myself.' Notable points: lì 'there'; era (impersonal/existential 'there was'); che introduces the restrictive clause; i' is the poet's contraction for io (here omitted in the provided lemma senses), mi son messo is the perfect construction ('I have put myself' / 'I set myself' = 'I set out'), with son (literary for sono) + past participle messo.
segments.purgatorio-1-g1: Per + infinitive (Per correr) expresses purpose = 'in order to'. 'Correr' is the infinitive used metaphorically ('to run/ride the waters'). 'Alza' is 3rd person singular present indicative ('hoists/raises'); the subject 'la navicella del mio ingegno' follows the verb (poetic inversion). 'Omai' is a temporal adverb placed before the subject meaning 'now'/'henceforth'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g2: 'Che' introduces a relative clause referring back to 'navicella'. 'Lascia' is present indicative 'leaves'; 'dietro a sé' is the idiom 'behind itself' (a marks the relation). 'Mar' is a poetic form of 'mare' (sea) and 'sì crudele' = 'so/cruel' with 'sì' as intensifier.
segments.purgatorio-1-g3: 'E canterò' = 'and I will sing': 'canterò' is 1st person singular future indicative (poetic future of the poet's utterance). 'Di quel secondo regno' = 'of that second realm' (di + demonstrative + noun). The subordinate 'dove l'umano spirito si purga' uses the reflexive 'si' to form a passive/mediopassive ('is purified' / 'purges itself'). The clause 'e di salire al ciel diventa degno' places the infinitive clause 'di salire al ciel' before the verb 'diventa degno' (diventa = present indicative 'becomes', 'degno' = adjective 'worthy'); 'di' marks the infinitive construction.
segments.purgatorio-1-g4: 'Ma qui' = 'but here'. 'La morta poesì' = 'dead poetry' (adjective after article); 'resurga' is present subjunctive 3rd person singular expressing a wish/optative ('may ... rise again'). The whole line is an invocation asking for the revival of poetry.
segments.purgatorio-1-g5: 'O' is the vocative interjection addressing the Muses; 'sante Muse' is the vocative plural with the adjective 'sante' agreeing in feminine plural.
segments.purgatorio-1-g6: 'Poi che' here functions as a causal conjunction equivalent to 'since/inasmuch as'. 'Vostro sono' is an inverted predication: 'I am yours' (vostro = predicative possessive; 'sono' is 1st person singular present indicative).
segments.purgatorio-1-g7: 'E qui Calïopè alquanto surga' continues the petition: 'surga' is present subjunctive 3rd person singular expressing a wish ('may Calliope arise'); 'alquanto' modifies the degree ('somewhat').
segments.purgatorio-1-g8: 'Seguitando' is a present participle/gerund meaning 'following' or 'accompanying' and links to the following clause. 'Il mio canto' is the object being followed. 'Con quel suono di cui' = 'with that sound of which' (di cui = 'of which'). 'Le Piche misere' is the subject of the verb that follows; 'sentiro' is an archaic past form (older orthography for sentirono, 3rd person plural past indicative) meaning 'felt' or 'perceived'. Note the poetic/inverted word order 'lo colpo tal' (the blow such).
segments.purgatorio-1-g9: This is a result clause introduced by 'che' = 'such that'. 'Disperar' is an infinitive meaning 'to despair' used impersonally here ('they despaired'); 'perdono' in context is the noun 'pardon/forgiveness' (the lexical list had verb senses, so here it functions as a noun meaning 'pardon'). The line says they 'despaired of pardon'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g10: A compact noun phrase: 'Dolce color d’orïental zaffiro' = 'sweet color of eastern sapphire'. The genitive 'd’oriental zaffiro' specifies the hue; the diaeresis over ï is a poetic orthography and does not affect grammar.
segments.purgatorio-1-g11: 'Che s’accoglieva' is a relative clause: 'che' = 'which'; 's’accoglieva' is imperfect indicative reflexive ('was being received' / 'was gathered') describing an ongoing state. 'Nel sereno aspetto del mezzo' is a locative phrase ('in the clear aspect of the middle'), with 'del mezzo' indicating 'of the middle/heaven'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g12: 'Puro infino al primo giro' = 'pure up to/as far as the first circuit/turn'; 'infino' is a preposition meaning 'as far as', and 'al primo giro' is a contracted preposition + article.
segments.purgatorio-1-g13: 'A li occhi miei' = 'to my eyes' (dative/location), placed before the verb for emphasis. 'Ricominciò' is passato remoto (simple past) 3rd person singular 'began again' or 'returned' and the subject is 'diletto' (delight) which follows the verb in inversion.
segments.purgatorio-1-g14: 'Tosto ch’io usci’ fuor de l’aura morta' = 'as soon as I went out of the dead air': 'tosto ch’' is a conjunction meaning 'as soon as'; 'usci’' is an archaic/past form meaning 'I went out' (past tense); 'fuor de' = 'out of' (old form of 'fuori da/di'). The relative clause 'che m’avea contristati li occhi e ’l petto' uses 'm’' = 'mi' (me), 'avea' = past indicative 'had', and 'contristati' is a past participle agreeing with the plural object 'li occhi e 'l petto' ('had saddened my eyes and chest').
segments.purgatorio-1-g15: 'Lo bel pianeto' = 'the fair/beautiful planet' (article + adjective + noun). The relative clause 'che d’amar conforta' uses 'd’amar' (infinitive 'to love') where 'conforta' (3rd person present indicative) means 'encourages' or 'inspires' — literally 'the planet that comforts/encourages love'. 'Faceva' is imperfect ('was making' / 'made' as a habitual or descriptive past) and governs the object phrase 'tutto rider l’orïente' = 'made the whole East smile' (poetic inversion: object before the place 'l'orïente').
segments.purgatorio-1-g16: 'Velando' is a gerund ('veiling/covering') showing a concurrent or resulting action. 'I Pesci' here refers to the constellation (Pisces) rather than literal fish, and 'ch’erano in sua scorta' is a relative clause with 'erano' imperfect indicative ('were') and 'sua scorta' = 'its escort/retinue' (the planet's attendant stars); the line means the planet hid/veiled the Fish that accompanied it.
segments.purgatorio-1-g124: This is a temporal clause introduced by Quando + passato remoto fummo ('when we were/arrived'); fummo is the narrative past used by Dante. 'là ’ve' is archaic contraction (’ve = ove) meaning 'there where'. 'la rugiada pugna col sole' uses pugna (present) as a vivid descriptive present: literally 'the dew fights/struggles with the sun', a poetic image subordinate to the initial temporal clause.
segments.purgatorio-1-g125: per + infinitive (per essere) expresses purpose/result here: 'so as to be' or 'in order to be'; 'in parte' functions locatively ('in a part/place'); 'dove' begins the relative clause that describes that place and ties to the following phrase.
segments.purgatorio-1-g126: 'ad orezza' is an archaic/rare adverbial phrase; ad + noun here gives a temporal nuance ('at orezza'), usually interpreted as 'at dawn' or 'at first light'. Dante places it parenthetically to qualify the time in the subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-1-g127: Impersonal/passive construction with si + present dirada: 'poco si dirada' = 'it is little thinned' or 'it disperses only a little'. 'poco' modifies the verb, so the clause says the dew is scarcely dispersed at that time.
segments.purgatorio-1-g128: Word order is inverted for poetic effect: the object/phrase 'ambo le mani in su l’erbetta sparte' (both hands up, spread on the little grass) comes before the verb pose (passato remoto of porre). 'soavemente' is an adverb ('softly/gently') modifying pose. 'in su' = 'up on/upon'; 'sparte' is a participial/adjectival past form 'spread'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g129: Ond' is a contracted form (onde/ond) meaning 'from which/whereupon' and introduces the consequence/result of the master's action; io is simply the subject 'I' that follows the connective in Dante's word order.
segments.purgatorio-1-g130: Relative clause with che introducing the speaker's state: 'che fui accorto di sua arte' uses fui + accorto (passato remoto + adjective/participial use) to mean 'I was aware / I had noticed his skill'. Di sua arte = 'of his art/skill' qualifies what was perceived.
segments.purgatorio-1-g131: porsi here is Dante's poetic form equivalent to 'mi posi' (passato remoto reflexive) meaning 'I turned/placed myself'; ver' is a contraction of verso (toward) and introduces the direction ('toward him'); the direct object 'le guance lagrimose' ('my tearful cheeks') follows the verb in normal order.
segments.purgatorio-1-g132: Ivi 'there' sets the location; 'mi fece' is a causative past (fece = he made) with mi as the object — 'he caused me to be ...'; 'tutto discoverto' is a predicate complement meaning 'completely uncovered/revealed'; the final relative clause 'quel color che l’inferno mi nascose' uses nascose (passato remoto of nascondere) to mean 'which Hell hid from me.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g133: Venimmo is the 1st-person plural past historic (venimmo) = “we came / we reached.” Poi = “then.” In sul = in + sul (“on the / to the”) and lito = “shore / strand.” Diserto = “deserted / unvisited.” This is a simple narrative clause describing arrival.
segments.purgatorio-1-g134: Che introduces a relative clause modifying lito. Mai non = “never” (mai combined with non). Vide is the past historic of vedere (“saw”). Navicar is an infinitive meaning “to sail / to navigate” (Dante’s form). Sue = suo/its, acque = “waters.” Omo = “man.” The relative clause says: the shore never saw a man sail its waters (i.e., none has successfully navigated them).
segments.purgatorio-1-g135: Che here resumes as a relative pronoun “who/that.” Di tornar = of returning / to return. Sia is the present subjunctive of essere (used in result/relative clauses), and poscia = afterwards. Esperto = past participle used adjectivally: “experienced / proven” in returning. The clause qualifies that no one who sailed those waters was later experienced in returning — i.e., none returned successfully.
segments.purgatorio-1-g136: Quivi is an adverb meaning 'there' (an archaic/poetic variant of qui). Mi is the unstressed clitic direct object 'me' placed before the verb; cinse is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of cingere, so 'it encircled/clasped me.' Sì com' is a folded comparative construction (sì ... come) with com' a contraction of come before a vowel, meaning 'in the way that'; altrui is an indirect/reflexive-style pronoun meaning 'another/someone else'; piacque is the passato remoto of piacere used impersonally ('it pleased'), so the whole phrase means 'in the manner that it pleased someone else.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g137: Oh is an exclamatory particle ('O, oh'); maraviglia (archaic spelling of meraviglia) is a feminine noun used as an interjection meaning 'wonder' or 'marvel.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g138: Ché is a causal conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because.' Qual here functions like 'as' or 'in the way that' introducing the manner clause ('as he chose'); elli is an archaic subject pronoun (historically used where modern Italian has egli/egli or lui) referring to the agent who chose (often a divine agent in context) — this form is archaic/poetic. Scelse is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of scegliere, 'he chose.' L' is the elided definite article before a vowel; umile pianta = 'humble/lowly plant.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g139: Cotal means 'such' or 'in such a way.' Si is the reflexive/clitic particle marking a passive or middle construction ('itself/it was'); rinacque is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of rinascere = 'was reborn' or 'came to life again.' Subitamente is an adverb 'suddenly/instantly'; là is an adverb 'there.' Onde is used here as a relative adverb meaning 'from where' or 'whence' (an archaic/poetic use). L' is the clitic direct object 'it' attached to the following verb; avelse is the passato remoto of avellere (to tear away/uproot) with the clitic already placed before it in the form l'avelse = 'had torn it away.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g111: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and sits before the verb for emphasis; sparì is passato remoto (third person singular) of sparire, a simple past tense used in poetry to mean 'vanished' or 'disappeared'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g112: The clause begins with the coordinating conjunction e 'and'. io (subject pronoun) is optional in Italian but here used for emphasis; sù (adverb) 'up' precedes the clitic mi (reflexive 'myself') and the verb levai, passato remoto first-person singular of levare, meaning 'I got up' or 'I rose'. sanza is an archaic form of senza 'without', and parlare is the infinitive 'to speak', so sanza parlare = 'without speaking'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g113: e 'and' links the actions; tutto here functions adverbially ('completely/altogether') modifying the reflexive action; mi is the reflexive clitic 'myself'; ritrassi is passato remoto first-person singular of ritrarsi/ritrassare 'to withdraw/retreat', so 'I drew back'; al = a + il 'to the'; duca mio = 'my duca' (Virgil as guide).
segments.purgatorio-1-g114: Again e 'and' coordinates the final action; li is the archaic masculine plural definite article (modern gli) used with occhi 'eyes'; a + lui = 'to him' (a with tonic pronoun lui); drizzai is passato remoto first-person singular of drizzare, here meaning 'I directed/turned (my eyes)' or 'I raised (my eyes)'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g115: El is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (modern lui/egli); cominciò is passato remoto third-person singular of cominciare, 'he began'. The colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-1-g116: Figliuol is a vocative 'son' (address), an archaic spelling of figliuolo/figlio, used here to call the addressee before giving an imperative.
segments.purgatorio-1-g117: segui is the second-person singular imperative of seguire, 'follow!' addressed to 'son'; i miei passi = 'my steps' where i is the plural definite article and miei is the possessive adjective 'my'; passi is plural 'steps' or 'paces.'
segments.purgatorio-1-g118: volgianci is an imperative form volgi (from volgere 'turn') with the enclitic ci attached, so 'turn us' or 'turn (with) us' addressed to the addressee; in is the preposition 'in' and dietro is an adverb 'back/behind', so the phrase means roughly 'turn (us) back' or 'turn about'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g119: ché is a causal conjunction 'for/because'; di qua means 'on this side' or 'on this side here' (di + qua); dichina is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'slopes/leans' (archaic usage) describing the plain; questa pianura = 'this plain'; a’ (a + i) suoi termini bassi = 'to its low limits' — a introduces the destination or extent and suoi agrees with pianura as 'its'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g120: L’alba (the article elided before a vowel) is the subject 'the dawn'; vinceva is imperfect indicative third-person singular of vincere, 'was prevailing/overcoming'; l’ora mattutina 'the morning hour' is the direct object; che introduces a relative clause; fuggia is a literary/poetic verbal form meaning 'was fleeing' or 'fled on' (Dante uses older subjunctive/indicative shapes), and innanzi is an adverb 'ahead/before', so the clause qualifies the hour as 'the morning hour that was fleeing ahead'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g121: sì che is a result clause 'so that'; di lontano 'from a distance' (di + lontano); conobbi is passato remoto first-person singular of conoscere used in the sense 'I recognized' or 'I made out'; il tremolar is a nominalized infinitive 'the trembling' or 'the shimmer' (tremolar = shimmering motion); de la marina is an archaic form of della marina 'of the sea'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g122: Noi 'we' is the subject; andavam is the imperfect first-person plural of andare used here as a past continuous 'we were going'; per lo solingo piano = 'over/through the solitary plain' (per + definite article lo + solingo 'lonely' + piano 'plain'); com’ is short for 'come' meaning 'as' or 'like'; om 'man' is an archaic form of uomo; che torna a la perduta strada is a relative clause 'who returns to the lost road' (torna is present or habitual 'returns' used in a simile), and perduta modifies strada 'lost road'.
segments.purgatorio-1-g123: che introduces a further relative/explanatory clause; ’nfino (nfino) is an archaic form of 'fino' with an initial n elided here meaning 'up to' or 'even up to'; ad essa 'to it' (the road); li is the archaic article (here marking the indirect object or dative sense in older usage); pare is third-person singular present of parere 'seems'; ire is the infinitive 'to go'; in vano is the adverbial phrase 'vain/pointless' so the clause means 'which makes it seem to him that even going up to it is in vain.'

sentences.purgatorio-1-s21: I have shown him all the wicked people; and now I mean to show those spirits who purge themselves under your charge.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s22: How I brought him would be long to tell you; from on high a power descends that helps me to bring him to see you and to hear you.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s23: Now may it please you to welcome his coming: he seeks liberty, which is so dear, as he who for her refuses life knows.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s24: You know it, for the death in Utica was not bitter to you on her account, where you left the robe that on the great day will be so bright.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s13: Are the laws of the abyss so broken?
sentences.purgatorio-1-s14: Or has a new counsel been adopted in heaven, that you, damned ones, come to my caverns?
sentences.purgatorio-1-s16: My guide then seized me, and with words, with hands, and with reverent gestures he set my legs and my eyelid moving.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s29: But if a lady of Heaven moves and guides you, as you said, there is no need of flattery: it suffices that, for her sake, she call me back.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s30: Go then, and see that you bind this fellow with a straight reed and wash his face, so that every filth is then wiped away; for it would not be fitting, his eye clouded by some mist, to go before the chief minister, who belongs to those of Paradise.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s31: This little islet, right around down to the very depths, down there where the wave strikes it, bears reeds upon the soft silt: no other plant that would put forth foliage or harden can live there, because it does not endure the blows.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s32: After that, your return will not be from here; only the sun — which is rising now — will show you how to take the mountain by a gentler ascent.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s25: The eternal edicts are not annulled for us, for this one lives and Minos does not bind me; but I belong to the circle where the chaste eyes of your Marzia are, who even now, at sight, beseeches you, O holy breast, that you keep her for your sake: therefore, for her love, incline yourself toward us.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s26: Let them go through your seven kingdoms; I will bring thanks from you to her, if those favors are worthy of being mentioned down there.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s27: "Marcia pleased my eyes so much while I was there," he then said, "that whatever favors she wished of me, I did."
sentences.purgatorio-1-s28: Now that he dwells over there beyond the evil river, he can no longer move me, by that law which was made when I went out from there.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s9: The rays of the four holy lights so adorned his face with light that I saw him as if the sun were before him.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s10: “Who are you who, fleeing the blind river, have escaped the eternal prison?”
sentences.purgatorio-1-s11: , he said, moving those honest feathers.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s12: “Who has guided you, or what was for you a light, coming forth out of the deep night that always makes the infernal valley black?”
sentences.purgatorio-1-s5: I turned to the right, and fixed my mind on the other pole, and I saw four stars never seen before except by the first people.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s6: The sky seemed to take pleasure in their little flames: O northern, widowed site, since you are deprived of seeing them!
sentences.purgatorio-1-s7: When I had withdrawn from their gaze, turning myself a little toward the other pole, where the Chariot had already disappeared, I saw near me an old man alone, worthy in appearance of such reverence that no son should (any longer) owe it to any father.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s8: He wore a long beard mixed with white hair, like his own hair, from which a double strip fell down upon his chest.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s17: Then he answered: "I did not come of my own accord: a lady descended from heaven, and by whose prayers this one was helped to my company."
sentences.purgatorio-1-s18: But since it is your will that more be explained of our condition as it truly is, my own cannot refuse you.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s19: He never saw the final evening; but by his folly it was so near to it that very little time remained to pass.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s20: As I said, I was sent to him in order to live by him; and there was no other way there than this by which I set myself (i.e. by which I set out).
sentences.purgatorio-1-s1: To make for better waters my little vessel of genius now hoists its sails, which leaves behind it so cruel a sea; and I will sing of that second realm where the human spirit is purified and becomes worthy to ascend to heaven.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s2: But here may dead poetry rise again, O holy Muses, since I am yours; and here let Calliope somewhat arise, following my song with that sound by which the wretched Piche felt so great a stroke that they despaired of pardon.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s3: Sweet color of oriental sapphire, which was gathered in the serene aspect of the middle, pure even to the first circuit; delight returned to my eyes, as soon as I went forth from the dead air that had saddened my eyes and my breast.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s4: The fair planet that encourages love made the whole Orient smile, veiling the Fish that were in its escort.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s37: When we were there where the dew struggles with the sun, so as to be in a place where, at dawn, it is little dispersed, my master softly placed both hands outspread upon the little grass; whereupon I, who had been aware of his art, turned my tearful cheeks toward him; there he completely revealed to me that color which Hell had hidden from me.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s38: We then came to the deserted strand, which no man has ever seen sail its waters and afterwards be experienced in returning.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s39: There it encircled me, just as it pleased someone else: O wonder!
sentences.purgatorio-1-s40: For as he chose the humble plant, so it was immediately reborn there from where it had been torn away.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s33: Thus he vanished; and I rose up without speaking, and drew myself completely back toward my guide, and fixed my eyes on him.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s34: He began: "Son, follow my steps; turn us back, for on this side this plain slopes toward its low limits."
sentences.purgatorio-1-s35: Dawn was conquering the morning hour that was fleeing before it, so that from afar I made out the shimmer of the sea.
sentences.purgatorio-1-s36: We were walking over the lonely plain like a man who returns to the lost road, which to him seems that even up to it the going is in vain.
